evals.name: spawn-time-reaction
evals.prompt: I'm working on a Bevy 0.19 game. Whenever a Creature entity dies, I want to spawn a particle effect at its position and play a death sound. How should I wire this up? Give me the recommended pattern with brief code.
expected_behavior: Recommends an EntityEvent (e.g. CreatureDied { entity }) plus an observer, OR an On<Remove, Creature>/On<Despawn, Creature> lifecycle observer — not the old EventReader/EventWriter approach.
Uses 0.17+ idioms: On<E> parameter (not Trigger<E>), commands.trigger() to fire, derive(EntityEvent) when the entity is the target.
Mentions reading the dying creature's Transform/position via the observer's system params before despawn fires (or stashing it in the event).
Does not suggest Event with EventReader, does not suggest world.trigger_targets.
Code uses MeshMaterial3d/Mesh3d wrappers if it touches material handles (does not use Query<&Handle<...>>).
If it mentions the replace/discard lifecycle event, uses the 0.19 name Discard (not Replace).

evals.name: cheatsheet-recall-handle
evals.prompt: I have this Bevy code and it stopped compiling: `fn pulse(query: Query<&Handle<StandardMaterial>>) { ... }`. The error says Handle<StandardMaterial> is not a Component. What changed and what's the modern way to write this?
expected_behavior: Identifies that the change was in 0.17 — Handle<T> is no longer a Component for materials/meshes.
Recommends Query<&MeshMaterial3d<StandardMaterial>>.
Explains that the underlying handle is accessed via .0 (or pattern destructuring) on the wrapper.
Optionally mentions related wrappers: Mesh3d, Mesh2d, MeshMaterial2d.
Does not invent a non-existent fix; does not suggest reverting to an older Bevy version.

evals.name: damage-batching
evals.prompt: Design a damage system for a Bevy 0.19 game. I have multiple sources of damage — melee attacks, ranged projectiles, environmental hazards (fire, poison) — each running in its own system. I want one place that applies all damage to entities and triggers death checks. What's the idiomatic structure?
expected_behavior: Recommends Message (#[derive(Message)] struct DamageDealt { target, amount, ... }) for the multi-producer/single-consumer batching pattern, NOT Event/Observer.
Uses MessageWriter in producer systems and MessageReader in the apply_damage consumer system.
Registers with app.add_message::<DamageDealt>() (not the old add_event).
Suggests separating death detection — either a separate DeathOccurred event/message fired from apply_damage, OR an Observer on Health changes.
Mentions ordering: damage producers in Update before apply_damage; apply_damage before death check (e.g., via system sets or .chain()).
Suggests one plugin per concern (CombatPlugin) registering all of this.

evals.name: state-machine-cleanup
evals.prompt: I'm setting up a Bevy 0.19 game with a main menu, gameplay, and game-over screen. How do I structure the states, and what's the cleanest way to make sure menus despawn when transitioning between them so I don't leak HUD entities?
expected_behavior: Defines a #[derive(States, ...)] enum with the three states; registers with app.init_state::<AppState>().
Uses OnEnter(AppState::X) / OnExit(AppState::X) for setup/teardown systems.
Recommends DespawnOnExit(AppState::X) on entities that should not survive the state — the canonical pattern that eliminates manual cleanup systems.
Optionally mentions sub-states for nested states (e.g. Paused inside InGame), or computed states for 'is the game in any of these states' run conditions.
Notes that next_state.set(X) re-fires OnEnter even if already in X, and that in 0.19 this also re-runs DespawnOnEnter/DespawnOnExit; set_if_neq for the skip-if-equal behavior.
Does not propose manually iterating queries to despawn entities one by one.

evals.name: plugin-organization
evals.prompt: I'm starting a new feature for a Bevy 0.19 game: an inventory system (items, equipment, slots). The codebase already has CombatPlugin and MovementPlugin. How should I organize the inventory code? What goes in the plugin, where do I put types, and how do I keep system ordering sane as more features arrive?
expected_behavior: Recommends one plugin per feature: an InventoryPlugin (struct or fn-as-plugin) that registers messages/observers/systems for inventory.
Recommends organizing by feature, not by file kind: src/inventory/{plugin,components,systems,messages}.rs (or similar), not src/components/inventory.rs + src/systems/inventory.rs.

Recommends a centralized SystemSet enum (configured once in app.rs/main.rs) with named ordering phases; plugins drop systems into named sets via .in_set(...) — does NOT recommend each plugin call configure_sets.
Uses #[require(...)] for required components when items always go together, rather than tuple bundles.
Mentions resources for plugin config when runtime tuning matters; plugin-struct fields only for build-time-only choices.
Mentions visibility hygiene — pub(crate) by default, pub for the plugin and a small public API of components/messages.

evals.name: resources-as-components
evals.prompt: I just upgraded my Bevy game to 0.19 and now `#[derive(Component, Resource)] struct GameConfig { ... }` won't compile — something about conflicting Component implementations. What changed and how do I fix it?
expected_behavior: Explains that in 0.19 Resource became a subtrait of Component, so #[derive(Resource)] now ALSO implements Component — deriving both gives two conflicting Component impls.

Recommends dropping the Component derive if it's only used as a resource (just #[derive(Resource)]), or splitting into two distinct types if both a component flavor and a resource flavor are genuinely needed.
Does NOT claim that co-deriving is fine, and does not invent a #[component]/#[resource] attribute to suppress the conflict.

May correctly mention related 0.19 consequences: #[reflect(Resource)] becoming a marker (use ReflectComponent for reflection), broad queries needing Without<IsResource>, or resources now supporting hooks/observers/immutability — but does not fabricate APIs.

evals.name: text-font-update
evals.prompt: My Bevy UI text code broke after upgrading to 0.19: `TextFont { font: asset_server.load("font.ttf"), font_size: 24.0, ..default() }` gives type errors on both font and font_size. What's the correct 0.19 form?
expected_behavior: Identifies that 0.19 migrated text to parley: TextFont.font is now a FontSource and font_size is now a FontSize enum.
Gives the fix: font: asset_server.load("font.ttf").into() (or FontSource::Family/FontSource::Monospace), font_size: FontSize::Px(24.0).
Does not claim the old f32 / Handle<Font> still works.
May correctly mention new weight/width/style fields, LetterSpacing, FontSize::Rem/Vh responsive units, or the system_font_discovery feature — without fabricating API names.

evals.name: gltf-scene-spawning
evals.prompt: In Bevy 0.19, `commands.spawn(SceneRoot(asset_server.load("hero.glb#Scene0")))` no longer compiles — SceneRoot isn't found. How do I spawn a glTF scene now?
expected_behavior: Explains that 0.19 renamed the old scene crate bevy_scene -> bevy_world_serialization (the bevy_scene name now hosts the new BSN system).
Gives the fix: commands.spawn(WorldAssetRoot(asset_server.load("hero.glb#Scene0"))).
Does not suggest BSN / bsn! / spawn_scene as the glTF spawning path (glTF is not ported to BSN yet).
May correctly mention related renames (DynamicScene -> DynamicWorld) or that direct material sub-asset loads now yield Handle<GltfMaterial>, without fabricating a /std suffix or other unverified API.

evals.name: text-input-widget
evals.prompt: I need a simple text entry field in my Bevy 0.19 game UI — a player-name input. What's the recommended way to do this in 0.19?
expected_behavior: Recommends the EditableText widget (new first-class text input in 0.19), spawned as a component on a Node entity.
Mentions it integrates with the InputFocus resource, and that InputFocus fields are private — use get()/set(entity, FocusCause::...)/clear().
Mentions reading the value via editable.value() and/or the TextEditChange event, and that bevy_ui_widgets is in default features now (no experimental feature flag needed).
May mention FeathersTextInput as the themed alternative, or TabNavigationPlugin/TabIndex for focus — without fabricating APIs.
Does not hand-roll keyboard-event parsing as the primary recommendation when EditableText exists.

evals.name: bsn-scene-composition
evals.prompt: I'm building a settings menu in Bevy 0.19 and heard about the new bsn! scene system. How do I define a reusable button widget and compose a small menu from it, and how do I get it into the world?
expected_behavior: Uses the bsn! macro to define scenes, with reusable scene fragments as functions returning impl Scene (e.g. fn button(label: &str) -> impl Scene).
Composes children with Children [ ... ] and attaches behavior with on(|e: On<...>, ...| { ... }) observers inline.
Spawns the scene with commands.spawn_scene(...) / world.spawn_scene(...) (or the fn.spawn() startup-system idiom) — NOT via a SceneRoot/WorldAssetRoot component (those are for the old/glTF system).
Does not claim .bsn files can be loaded yet, and does not claim glTF uses BSN — both are explicitly not ready in 0.19.
Does not fabricate BSN syntax; patches, :fn inheritance (arg-less), and @SceneComponent { @prop } usage (if shown) match the real macro.

evals.name: light-shadow-fields
evals.prompt: After upgrading my Bevy game to 0.19, `PointLight { shadows_enabled: true, ..default() }` no longer compiles — no field `shadows_enabled`. What changed?
expected_behavior: Explains that 0.19 renamed shadows_enabled -> shadow_maps_enabled on PointLight (and DirectionalLight/SpotLight), because lights now also support contact shadows.
Gives the fix: PointLight { shadow_maps_enabled: true, ..default() }.
May correctly mention the new contact_shadows_enabled field (and that contact shadows need a ContactShadows component on the camera) without fabricating other APIs.
Does not suggest reverting Bevy or invent an unrelated fix.
